---
title: "Ponceau 4R (INS 124)"
slug: ponceau-4r-ins-124
category: "Additives & Functional"
category-slug: additives-functional
keywords: ["Ponceau 4R", "INS 124", "Synthetic Food Colour", "Azo Dye", "Red Colourant", "Food Additive", "FMCG"]
description: "Ponceau 4R (INS 124) is a synthetic azo dye, named for its poppy-red colour"
---

## History and Sourcing

[Ponceau 4R (INS 124)](ponceau-4r-ins-124.qmd) is a synthetic azo dye, named for its poppy-red colour. It lacks historical or traditional culinary roots in India, being a product of modern chemical synthesis rather than natural sourcing. Its introduction to India is tied to the growth of the industrial food processing sector, where it serves as a vibrant food colouring agent.

## Home Kitchen Use

In traditional Indian home cooking, Ponceau 4R is not used. Home kitchens historically rely on natural colourants from ingredients like turmeric, saffron, beetroot, or chilli powder. Therefore, this synthetic additive does not feature in authentic, traditional Indian recipes.

## Industrial Applications

Ponceau 4R is widely utilized in the Indian FMCG sector due to its stability, intense colour, and cost-effectiveness. It is commonly found in confectionery (candies, jellies), beverages (soft drinks, flavoured drinks), dairy products (flavoured yogurts, ice creams), and various processed snack foods and savouries, where it enhances visual appeal as an E-COLOR additive.

## Distinctions and Common Confusion

Ponceau 4R is often confused with natural red colours or other synthetic dyes. It is distinct from natural pigments like beetroot red or lycopene due to its synthetic origin and consistent, bright red hue. While similar to other synthetic red azo dyes such as [Allura Red (INS 129)](allura-red-ins-129.qmd) or [Carmoisine (INS 122)](carmoisine-ins-122.qmd), each possesses a unique chemical structure, specific shade, and distinct regulatory approvals for various food applications, making them non-interchangeable.
